# 

# **Purpose of this document** {#purpose-of-this-document}

This document serves as the formal briefing from Maverx to Digilize and the participating student teams. It outlines what Maverx will deliver as input, what we expect as output, and what we deliberately leave open for the teams to define themselves. The goal is to ensure all parties start with a shared and unambiguous understanding of the assignment, so that teams can focus their energy on building the best possible solution. 

1. # **What we want to build** {#what-we-want-to-build}

**The product vision:**  
An AI-powered training builder that takes a user from a rough idea to a fully structured, styled PowerPoint presentation, through a guided intake process. The system is not a content generator that produces random slides. It is a structured production assistant that follows a fixed didactic model Maverx uses across all its trainings. The human remains in control of quality; the system handles structure, consistency and production speed.

**The problem it solves:**  
Today, developing a new training at Maverx takes a lot of time. A significant portion of that time is spent on tasks that are largely repeatable: defining a logical structure, formatting slides, applying house style, writing introductory and wrap-up content. This limits how quickly Maverx can respond to client demand and how consistently quality is maintained across different trainers.

**What success looks like:**  
A trainer enters a single-sentence description of a training need. Before any content is generated, the system offers two upfront options: (1) the trainer provides their own outline for the training, or (2) the system gathers relevant input on the topic itself and then jointly arrives at an outline with the trainer.  
From there, the system works iteratively. Rather than asking ten questions upfront about a single slide, it walks through the training in logical chunks (e.g. per theory block) and confirms its approach with the trainer before producing each part — "this is my proposal, this is how I'll build it, agree or adjust?". The trainer stays in control of direction; the system handles structure, consistency and production speed.  
The end result is a complete, editable PowerPoint presentation in Maverx house style, ready for content review and final touches.

2. # **How it should work** {#how-it-should-work}

User input (single-sentence training need)

↓

Choice of outline path:

   (a) trainer provides their own outline, OR

   (b) research agent gathers input and proposes an outline jointly with the trainer

↓

Agreed training outline (modules, timing, sequence)

↓

Iterative content generation — per logical chunk (e.g. theory block, exercise), the system proposes its approach, the trainer agrees or adjusts, then the chunk is built

↓

Output: editable .pptx in Maverx house style

**The intake phase**  
The system opens with a short conversational intake to gather enough context to choose an outline path and frame the training. The trainer is then offered two options before any content is generated:

* Option A — Trainer-supplied outline. The trainer provides their own outline for the training, and the system uses it as the structural basis.  
* Option B — Research-agent-assisted outline. The system uses a research agent to gather relevant input on the topic and proposes an outline, which the trainer reviews and adjusts until both agree.

Once the outline is agreed, the system moves into iterative content generation: it works through the training chunk by chunk (e.g. per theory block or exercise), and for each chunk presents its proposed approach to the trainer before producing the actual slides. The trainer stays in control of direction at every step.

Regardless of the path chosen, the following context must be established up front:

| Question | Why it matters |
| :---- | :---- |
| What is the topic or skill to be trained? | Defines the domain and content direction |
| Who is the Target Audience? | Determines tone, depth and examples |
| What is the knowledge level of participants? | Beginner / intermediate / advanced |
| How long is the training? | Determines number of modules and slide count |
| What is the primary learning objective? | Anchors the entire structure  |

The system may ask follow-up questions if any of this context is ambiguous. It should not move on to outline selection until the intake context is sufficiently complete, and should not start producing slides for a chunk until the trainer has confirmed the proposed approach for that chunk. 

**The training structure;**

| Block | Purpose |
| :---- | :---- |
| Opening / Kick-off | Set learning goals, introduce agenda |
| Theory Block(s)  | Core concepts, explained clearly for the target audience |
| Example(s) | Concrete, recognizable illustrations of the theory |
| Exercise(s) | Active application – individual, pair or group work |
| Wrap up / closing | Summarizes key takeaways, link to practice, next steps  |

**The output:**

* Format: .pptx — fully editable in Microsoft PowerPoint  
* Layout: based on the Maverx master slides (provided)  
* Content per slide: title \+ full content of slides (with assets delivered)  
* The presentation should be immediately usable as a working draft — not a finished product, but a strong first version that requires only content review

3. # **What Maverx will deliver to the teams**  {#what-maverx-will-deliver-to-the-teams}

| Item | Format | Purpose |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Style Guide | PDF | Colors, fonts, logo’s etc. |
| Master slides | .pptx | All slide layout in Maverx House Style |

4. # **MVP breakdown** 

|  | Tier 1 |
| :---- | ----- |
| **Format** | Single training |
| **Example** | Prompt Engineering |
| **PPT output** | 1 deck (30-50 slides) |
| **Pre & post bites** | Required |
| **Speaker notes** | Required |
| **Exercises** | Required |
| **Handout** | Bonus  |
| **Mentimeter** | Bonus |
| **Cross-session logic** | — |

Generate a single, complete training on a given topic for a specific target audience.  
Example prompt: *"Create a 3-hour training on Prompt Engineering for the Marketing team of a mid-sized company. Participants have no prior AI experience."*

*Minimum deliverables:*

* Intake via targeted questions (audience, level, duration, learning objective)  
* Generated training structure following the Maverx didactic model (kick-off → theory → example → exercise → wrap-up)  
* Full editable .pptx in Maverx house style, including text, tables and visuals — not placeholder screenshots  
* Pre-bite — a short preparation document for participants before the session (e.g. an article to read, software to install, or a reflection question)  
* Post-bite — a follow-up document after the session (e.g. reflection questions, a follow-up assignment, or further reading)  
* Speaker notes —   
  * per slide, a structured facilitation guide for the trainer including:    
    * Aim — one clear sentence on purpose  
    * Time — short estimate  
    * Instructions — conversational, trainer-ready, what to say and do step by step  
    * Key discussion points — the 3-4 things that must land  
    * Link to reality — a concrete story or analogy that makes it stick  
    * Debrief & Summary — one punchy closing line

**Things to keep in mind:**

* *Cost-efficient generation —* the end-to-end cost of generating a full training (from initial input to finished .pptx) must be kept as low as possible. Teams must measure and report the cost per generated training; lower cost is better.  
* *Output reliability score —* the system must produce a reliability or confidence indicator per slide showing how trustworthy its content is, so the trainer knows which slides need closer review.  
* *Bilingual output —* the generated training (slides, speaker notes, pre-bite and post-bite) must be available in both Dutch and English.  
* *Robustness against house style changes* — the Maverx house style is not static. Fonts, colors, logo's, master slides and other brand assets will evolve over time. The system must be built so that when we update our house style, swapping in the new assets is straightforward and does not require rebuilding the system. House style elements should be configurable inputs, not hard-coded assumptions.

5. # **What will be measured** {#what-will-be-measured}

Evaluation criteria in detail (these are also things we would like to see in the grading rubric):

| \# | Criterion | What we look at |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1 | Quality of pre / iterative questions asked  | Are the right questions asked? Does the system handle vague input gracefully? |
| 2 | Structural logic | Is the generated training outline didactically sound and properly sequenced (theory, examples, exercise?)  |
| 3 | House Style Compliance | Are Maverx master slides used correctly? Fonts, colors, layout intact?  |
| 4 | Output editability | Can we open and edit the .pptx without issues in standard PowerPoint (so no screenshots inserted into the PPT but adjustable text / tables etc.) |
| 5 | Transferability & Documentation | Is the solution understandable and usable without the team that built it? This means that we want to adjust the system when needed and also insert ‘new files’ as examples or something like that  |
| 6 | Output reliability & confidence scoring  | Does the system produce a reliability or confidence score indicating how trustworthy its content is? Is the score visible to the trainer, well-calibrated (low score on uncertain or fabricated content, high score on well-grounded content), and actionable so the trainer knows which slides need closer review? |
| 7 | Cost-efficient generation | What is the end-to-end cost (e.g. API / token cost) of generating one full training from input to finished .pptx? Teams must measure and report this cost, and lower cost is better. This is both a hard requirement (must be measured) and a grading dimension. |
| 8 | Bilingual output (NL \+ EN) | Can the system produce the full training (slides, speaker notes, pre-bite and post-bite) in both Dutch and English? Is the language consistent across all materials within one run? |

6. # **What we deliberately leave open** {#what-we-deliberately-leave-open}

The following is entirely up to the teams. We will not assess on technical choices, only on results:

* Which AI model(s) or APIs are used  
* Whether the interface is a chat, a web form, a script or something else   
* How the PowerPoint is generated technically

7. # **Extra deliverables?**

To ensure the challenge can be adapted to different student skill levels, we define three levels of increasing scope and complexity. The baseline of the assignment is the generation of a single training (Tier 1). However, depending on the level of the participating students, the challenge can be extended with additional layers of complexity. These extensions move beyond a single training towards multi-level modules or full learning tracks, introducing more advanced requirements in terms of structure, consistency and learning design. 

The table below provides an overview of the different levels and their expected outputs. A more detailed description per level is provided in the following section.

|  | Tier 2 | Tier 3 |
| :---- | ----- | ----- |
| **Format** | 3-level module | Multi-session track |
| **Example** | Power BI E/A/E | Lean Black Belt (8×2hr) |
| **PPT output** | 3 decks | 8 decks |
| **Pre & post bites** | Required | Required |
| **Speaker notes** | Required | Required |
| **Exercises \+ handout** | Required | Required \+ case handout |
| **Mentimeter** | Bonus | Required |
| **Cross-session logic** | Level progression | Framework backbone (DMAIC)  |

**Tier 2 — Multi-level Module**  
Generate a complete three-level learning track on a single tool or topic: Essentials, Advanced and Expert. The levels must build on each other — Expert assumes knowledge from Essentials and Advanced.

Example topics:

* Power Automate Essentials / Advanced / Expert  
* Power BI Essentials / Advanced / Expert  
* Claude / AI Literacy Essentials / Advanced / Expert  
* Excel Essentials / Advanced / Expert

Expected deliverables per level:

* Same as Tier 1  
* Mentimeter in session 2 and 3 to recap the previous session

*Bonus:*

- For tool based trainings, develop and create the datasets (adjustable for different participants  


The key challenge: the system must understand that level 2 presupposes level 1\. Content, exercises and difficulty must scale logically across the three modules.

**Tier 3 — Certification Track**  
Generate a full multi-session certification training spread across multiple sessions with a coherent narrative thread running through the entire programme.

Example:

* Lean Black Belt: 8 sessions of 2 hours, structured along the DMAIC framework  
* Each session covers a defined phase or set of tools  
* Sessions build on each other — the post-bite of session 3 connects directly to the pre-bite of session 4

Expected deliverables per session:

* Same as Tier 1 but including a full case handout  
  * With a realistic business case, the relevant model(s) as reference material, and space for participants to apply the framework themselves

Expected at track level:

* A logical structure across all sessions (e.g. DMAIC as the backbone across 8 sessions)  
* A consistent case running through the full track — participants work on the same fictional company or problem throughout, building on their work each session  
* A session overview document showing the red thread, timing and learning objectives per session

The key challenge: the system must maintain narrative and didactic consistency across 16 hours of training material. This is the most complex version of the assignment and represents the full vision of what Maverx ultimately wants to build.
